title: ser vs estar (to be)
explanationMd: Spanish splits 'to be' into two verbs. **ser** is for identity and permanent traits: who/what something is — origin, profession, personality, time, possession. **estar** is for states and location: how/where something is right now — feelings, conditions, position. The intuition: ser answers *what it is*, estar answers *how/where it is*. Both are wildly irregular and must be memorized: **ser** → soy, eres, es, somos, son; **estar** → estoy, estás, está, estamos, están. Some adjectives flip meaning: *es aburrido* (he's boring) vs *está aburrido* (he's bored); *es rico* (he's rich) vs *está rico* (it's tasty). Location of things/people is ALWAYS estar (*el baño está allá*) — but a scheduled event uses ser (*la fiesta es en mi casa*).

commonErrors.note: Tiredness is a state → estar.

commonErrors.wrong: El baño es a la derecha.
commonErrors.right: El baño está a la derecha.
commonErrors.note: Location always takes estar.

drills.prompt: 'She is a doctor' — ser or estar?

title: Gender and articles: el/la/los/las, un/una
explanationMd: Every noun is masculine or feminine. Articles carry it: **el/un** (m.), **la/una** (f.), plural **los/unos**, **las/unas**. Rough rule: -o → masculine, -a → feminine — but learn each noun WITH its article, because the exceptions are common and load-bearing: *el día, el problema, el mapa, el agua, la mano, la foto*. Words from Greek ending -ma are usually masculine (el problema, el tema, el clima). A feminine noun starting with a stressed *a* takes 'el' in the singular for sound but stays feminine: *el agua fría*. Unlike English, articles rarely drop: 'I like coffee' = *me gusta **el** café*.

commonErrors.note: -ma Greek words are masculine: el problema, el tema, el clima.
mano is feminine despite -o; the adjective agrees feminine.

title: Present tense: regular -ar, -er, -ir verbs
explanationMd: Three verb families, three ending sets. Drop the ending, add: **-ar** (hablar) → o, as, a, amos, an; **-er** (comer) → o, es, e, emos, en; **-ir** (vivir) → o, es, e, imos, en. Note -er and -ir differ only in the nosotros form (comemos vs vivimos). Because the ending already shows the subject, Spanish usually **drops the subject pronoun**: *hablo español* (not 'yo hablo español') unless you're emphasizing or contrasting. The present also covers English's 'am ___ing': *como* = I eat OR I'm eating.

commonErrors.note: Don't add 'soy' — the verb ending already means 'I'; drop the pronoun too.
nosotros always takes -amos/-emos/-imos.

title: Key irregular & stem-changing verbs: tener, ir, hacer, poder, querer
explanationMd: A handful of high-frequency verbs break the rules and carry daily conversation. **tener** (have): tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tienen — also for age/hunger/cold (see next point). **ir** (go): voy, vas, va, vamos, van — and the everyday future (ir a + inf). **hacer** (do/make): hago, haces, hace… — also weather. **poder** (can): puedo, puedes… and **querer** (want): quiero, quieres… are 'stem-changers' — the e/o in the stem becomes ie/ue in all forms except nosotros (podemos, queremos). This 'boot' pattern (nosotros stays regular) repeats across dozens of verbs: dormir→duermo, pensar→pienso, pedir→pido.

commonErrors.note: tener's yo form is irregular: tengo.
Stem-changers DON'T change in nosotros — it stays podemos, queremos.

title: tener: where Spanish 'has' instead of 'is'
explanationMd: A whole family of states that English does with 'to be', Spanish does with **tener** (to have): **tener hambre** (be hungry), **tener sed** (be thirsty), **tener frío/calor** (be cold/hot), **tener sueño** (be sleepy), **tener miedo** (be afraid), **tener prisa** (be in a hurry), **tener razón** (be right), and crucially **tener … años** (be … years old). Translating these with 'ser/estar' — *soy hambre*, *soy 25 años* — is the most recognizable beginner error in Spanish. Intensify with 'mucho/mucha' (a noun!), not 'muy': *tengo **mucha** hambre*, never 'muy hambre'.

commonErrors.note: Age always uses tener.
Hunger is a noun here, so it takes mucha, not muy.

title: gustar: liking things backwards
explanationMd: **gustar** doesn't mean 'to like' — it means 'to be pleasing'. So the grammar runs backwards from English: *me gusta el café* literally = 'coffee is pleasing to me'. The thing liked is the SUBJECT, and it decides the verb: singular thing → **gusta**, plural things → **gustan**. *Me gusta el café* / *me gustan los tacos*. The person is an indirect pronoun up front: **me, te, le, nos, les** gusta(n). For emphasis or clarity, add 'a + person': *a mí me gusta, a Juan le gusta*. Whole family works this way: **encantar** (love), **doler** (hurt — *me duele la cabeza*), **faltar** (be missing), **importar** (matter), and Mexican **antojarse** (crave — *se me antoja*).

examples.translation: I like coffee. (coffee is pleasing to me)

commonErrors.note: You don't 'do' the liking — the coffee is pleasing TO you: me gusta.
The verb agrees with the plural thing liked: gustan.

drills.prompt: 'I like the beach': Me ___ la playa.

title: Negation: no, and the double negative
explanationMd: Basic negation is simple: put **no** right before the verb — *no hablo español*, *no me gusta*. Object pronouns stay glued to the verb, so 'no' goes before them too: *no lo sé*, *no me gusta*. Spanish then does something English forbids: the **double negative** is correct and required. With words like **nunca** (never), **nada** (nothing), **nadie** (nobody), **ninguno** (none), **tampoco** (neither), you keep 'no' before the verb AND the negative word after: *no veo **nada*** (I don't see anything, lit. 'I don't see nothing'), *no viene **nadie***. You can drop the 'no' only if the negative word comes first: *nunca como carne* = *no como carne nunca*.

commonErrors.note: After 'no', use the negative 'nada', not 'algo'. Double negatives are correct in Spanish.
'no' goes before the pronoun+verb unit: no me gusta.

title: Asking questions and the accented question words
explanationMd: The easiest news in Spanish grammar: to make a yes/no question, **just change your intonation** (and add the opening ¿). *Hablas español* → *¿Hablas español?* No word reordering, no auxiliary 'do'. You can also flip the subject after the verb: *¿Habla usted inglés?* Question words all carry a written accent to distinguish them from their plain twins: **qué** (what), **quién** (who), **dónde** (where), **cuándo** (when), **cómo** (how), **cuánto** (how much), **por qué** (why), **cuál** (which). The opening ¿ warns the reader a question is coming — always paired with the closing ?. In speech, '¿Cómo?' or the Mexican '¿Mande?' is the polite 'what?/pardon?'.

commonErrors.note: Spanish requires the opening ¿ — it's not optional in writing.
Question words take an accent: qué, dónde, cómo…

title: tú vs usted — and ustedes for everyone
explanationMd: Spanish has two singular 'you'. **tú** is familiar (friends, family, peers, kids); **usted** is polite/respectful (strangers, elders, service, professional settings). Usted takes **third-person** verb forms — *usted habla*, *¿cómo está usted?* — the same as él/ella. In Mexico, usted is used more readily than in Spain: with older people, in shops, and as a sign of respect you can't go wrong defaulting to it with anyone you don't know. Two big Mexican notes: (1) there is **no vosotros** — the plural 'you' is always **ustedes** (with -an/-en verb endings), for both formal and casual groups. (2) Mexican voseo doesn't exist; that 'vos' is Argentine/Central American. So the system you actually use is: tú / usted / ustedes.

examples.translation: How are you? (to a friend — tú)
How are you? (to an older man — usted)
Do you (all) want something to drink? (plural = ustedes)

commonErrors.note: Mexico uses ustedes for all plural 'you' — vosotros isn't used.
usted takes third-person verb forms (habla), not tú forms (hablas).

drills.prompt: Ask an older stranger their name (usted):
Ask a group where they're from:
Turn 'tú tienes' into the usted form:

title: Adjective agreement and placement
explanationMd: Adjectives agree in gender and number: *un coche rojo, una casa roja, unos coches rojos, unas casas rojas*. Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant don't change for gender (grande, azul, fácil) — only add -s/-es for plural. **Placement: adjectives normally FOLLOW the noun** — *comida mexicana*, *un café caliente* — the reverse of English. A few short common ones can go before (bueno→buen, malo→mal, grande→gran drop endings before the noun), and placement can shift meaning: *un amigo viejo* (an elderly friend) vs *un viejo amigo* (a longtime friend). Nationality and color always follow.

examples.translation: It's a good restaurant. (bueno → buen before noun)

commonErrors.note: Colors follow the noun in Spanish.
The adjective agrees with feminine 'comida'.

drills.prompt: Place it: 'a good place' → un ___ lugar.

drills.answer: una ciudad grande (or 'una gran ciudad' = a great city)

title: Possessives: mi/tu/su and agreement
explanationMd: Possessives agree with the **thing owned**, not the owner, and mostly just add -s for plural: **mi/mis** (my), **tu/tus** (your, tú), **su/sus** (his/her/its/your-usted/their), **nuestro/a/os/as** (our — the only one that also changes for gender). So *su casa* can mean his, her, your (usted), or their house — context decides, and Spanish rarely disambiguates. Note *tu* (your) has no accent; *tú* (you) does. For extra clarity or emphasis, Spanish often uses 'de + person' instead: *la casa de Juan*, *el celular de ella*.

examples.text: Es el coche de mi hermano.

commonErrors.note: Possessives agree with the thing owned: one house → mi casa.
'tu' (your) has no accent; 'tú' (you) does.

title: The everyday future: ir a + infinitive
explanationMd: Just like English 'going to': conjugate **ir** (voy, vas, va, vamos, van) + **a** + infinitive. *Voy a comer* = I'm going to eat. This handles nearly all future talk in conversation; the one-word future (*comeré*) can wait until B1. Note **vamos a + infinitive** does double duty: 'we're going to ___' AND 'let's ___' — *vamos a comer* = we're going to eat / let's eat. Negation wraps the 'ir': *no voy a ir*. The 'a' is required and never contracts here (unlike a + el = al elsewhere).

examples.translation: What are you going to do tomorrow?
We're going to eat tacos. / Let's eat tacos.
It's not going to rain today.

commonErrors.note: The 'a' is required: ir A + infinitive.
The second verb stays in the infinitive: comer, not como.

title: The preterite: the everyday past
explanationMd: For completed past events, Mexican Spanish leans heavily on the **preterite** (where Spain often uses the present perfect). Regular endings: **-ar** → é, aste, ó, amos, aron (hablé, hablaste, habló…); **-er/-ir** → í, iste, ió, imos, ieron (comí, comiste, comió…). The accented endings matter: *hablo* (I speak) vs *habló* (he spoke). A cluster of irregulars must be learned as vocabulary — they share a special unaccented pattern: **ir/ser** → fui, fuiste, fue (identical for both!); **hacer** → hice, hiciste, hizo; **tener** → tuve; **estar** → estuve; **poder** → pude; **decir** → dije. In Mexico, '¿ya comiste?' and 'fui al mercado' are how the recent past is normally said.

examples.translation: Yesterday I ate at a restaurant.
Have you eaten? (lit. did you already eat)

examples.text: Fui al mercado en la mañana.
examples.translation: I went to the market in the morning.
What did you do this weekend?

commonErrors.note: A finished past event needs the preterite: hablé, with the accent.
ir and ser share the exact same preterite (fui, fuiste, fue); context tells them apart.

title: The imperfect: the background past (introduction)
explanationMd: Spanish has two everyday past tenses with different jobs. **Preterite** = what happened, a completed event: *comí* (I ate). **Imperfect** = the background — how things used to be, ongoing states, repeated habits, descriptions, and telling time/age in the past. Endings are refreshingly regular: **-ar** → aba, abas, aba, ábamos, aban (hablaba); **-er/-ir** → ía, ías, ía, íamos, ían (comía, vivía). Only three verbs are irregular: **ser** → era, **ir** → iba, **ver** → veía. At A2, produce the workhorses — *era* (it was), *había* (there was/were), *estaba* (was, state), *hacía* (weather was) — and recognize the rest. *Cuando era niño…* (when I was a kid…) is the classic imperfect opener.

examples.text: Cuando era niño, vivía en Guadalajara.
examples.translation: When I was a kid, I lived in Guadalajara.

examples.text: Había mucha gente en la fiesta.
examples.translation: There were a lot of people at the party.

examples.text: Estaba cansado, así que me quedé en casa.
examples.translation: I was tired, so I stayed home. (imperfect state + preterite event)

commonErrors.wrong: Cuando era niño, fui a la playa todos los veranos.
commonErrors.right: Cuando era niño, iba a la playa todos los veranos.
commonErrors.note: A repeated past habit takes the imperfect (iba), not the preterite.

commonErrors.wrong: Ayer hacía calor y comía tacos. (for the meal event)
commonErrors.right: Ayer hacía calor y comí tacos.
commonErrors.note: The single event 'ate' is preterite (comí); the background weather is imperfect (hacía).

title: Object pronouns: direct (lo/la) and indirect (le)
explanationMd: Object pronouns go BEFORE the conjugated verb: *lo veo* (I see it/him), *te llamo* (I'll call you). **Direct** (the thing acted on): me, te, **lo/la**, nos, **los/las** — *¿la cuenta? — ya la pedí*. **Indirect** ('to/for' someone): me, te, **le**, nos, **les** — *le hablo a Juan* (I talk to Juan). With an infinitive or gerund you can attach the pronoun to the end instead: *voy a llamarte = te voy a llamar*; *estoy haciéndolo = lo estoy haciendo*. When both pronouns appear, indirect comes first, and le/les becomes **se** before lo/la: *se lo doy* (I give it to him). A very Mexican habit: 'redundant' le — *¿le echamos ganas?*, *ándale*.

examples.translation: Do you see the metro? — Yes, I see it.

examples.text: Le di el dinero a mi hermano.
examples.translation: I gave the money to my brother.

examples.text: Voy a comprarlo. / Lo voy a comprar.
examples.translation: I'm going to buy it. (attached or before)

commonErrors.note: Object pronouns go before the conjugated verb (or attached to an infinitive: verlo).
le becomes 'se' before lo/la: se lo doy.

title: Reflexive verbs: llamarse, levantarse, sentirse
explanationMd: Daily routine runs on reflexive verbs, marked by -se on the infinitive. The pronoun changes with the subject: **me, te, se, nos, se** + verb — *me levanto, te levantas, se levanta, nos levantamos, se levantan*. The most-used of all is **llamarse** (to be called): *me llamo Ana*. Reflexives cover getting-ready verbs (levantarse, bañarse, vestirse), feelings (sentirse, preocuparse, enojarse), and many where English uses no pronoun (irse = to leave, quedarse = to stay). Some verbs change meaning with se: *ir* (go) → *irse* (leave, go away); *dormir* (sleep) → *dormirse* (fall asleep). The pronoun sits before the conjugated verb or attaches to an infinitive: *me voy a levantar = voy a levantarme*.

examples.translation: My name is Diego. (lit. I call myself)

commonErrors.note: It's reflexive: me llamo (I call myself). Without 'me' it means 'I call (someone)'.
Without 'me', levantar means to lift something else.

title: por vs para — the two 'for's
explanationMd: Both translate as 'for', but they split the work. **para** points forward — purpose, destination, recipient, deadline: *para ti* (for you), *para comer* (in order to eat), *para el lunes* (by Monday), *voy para el centro* (heading downtown). **por** points to cause, exchange, duration, and movement-through: *gracias por todo* (thanks for everything), *por el tráfico* (because of traffic), *por la mañana* (during the morning), *dos por cien pesos* (two for a hundred pesos), *paso por ti* (I'll come by/through for you). Rough test: **para** = goal/destination, **por** = reason/means/exchange. This pair takes time; learn it through fixed phrases first (por favor, por fin, por eso, para siempre) and the logic settles in.

examples.translation: This gift is for you. (recipient → para)
I'm studying for the exam. (goal → para)
I was late because of traffic. (reason → por)

commonErrors.note: Thanks for a cause/reason uses por.

commonErrors.wrong: Estudio por el examen. (meaning 'in order to pass it')
commonErrors.note: Purpose/goal uses para.

title: saber vs conocer — two ways to know
explanationMd: Spanish splits 'to know'. **saber** = know facts, information, or how to do something (a skill): *sé la respuesta*, *no sé*, *¿sabes nadar?* (do you know how to swim?). **conocer** = be familiar/acquainted with people, places, and works: *conozco a María*, *conozco un buen restaurante*. Both have an irregular yo form: **sé** (saber), **conozco** (conocer). Two tells: if 'know' is followed by a fact, a clause ('know that…'), or 'know how to + verb' → saber; if it's followed by a person or place → conocer (and people take the personal 'a': *conozco **a** Juan*). In the preterite, *conocí* means 'I met (for the first time)' and *supe* means 'I found out'.

examples.translation: I don't know where it is. (fact → saber)
Do you know how to cook? (skill → saber)

examples.text: Conozco un buen lugar para comer.
examples.translation: I know a good place to eat. (place → conocer)
I met her yesterday. (preterite conocer = met)

commonErrors.note: A fact/answer is known with saber.
Knowing a person is conocer, with the personal 'a'.

drills.answer: Conozco bien la Ciudad de México.

drills.prompt: saber or conocer: 'I don't know his name.'

title: Comparatives and superlatives: más/menos … que
explanationMd: Comparison is regular: **más … que** (more than), **menos … que** (less than), **tan … como** (as … as): *el metro es más rápido que el camión*. Superlative adds the article and uses 'de' where English says 'in': **el/la más …** — *el mejor restaurante **de** la ciudad*. Four irregulars replace 'más + adjective': **mejor** (better), **peor** (worse), **mayor** (older/bigger), **menor** (younger/smaller) — never 'más bueno/malo' for quality. Mexican Spanish also loves the **-ísimo** superlative suffix: *buenísimo* (super good), *carísimo* (crazy expensive), *padrísimo* (super cool) — an everyday way to say 'extremely'.

examples.text: El metro es más rápido que el camión.
examples.translation: The metro is faster than the bus.

examples.text: Es el mejor taco de la ciudad.
examples.translation: It's the best taco in the city.

examples.text: Esta salsa es menos picante que la otra.
examples.translation: This salsa is less spicy than the other one.

commonErrors.note: 'Better' is the irregular mejor, not 'más bueno'.

commonErrors.wrong: el mejor taco en la ciudad
commonErrors.right: el mejor taco de la ciudad
commonErrors.note: Spanish uses 'de', not 'en', in superlatives ('the best … in').

drills.answer: La cerveza es más barata que el vino.

title: este / ese / aquel — this, that, and that-over-there
explanationMd: Spanish has a three-way distance system where English has two. **este/esta/estos/estas** = this/these (near me). **ese/esa/esos/esas** = that/those (near you). **aquel/aquella/aquellos/aquellas** = that/those over there (far from both). They agree with the noun: *este taco, esa salsa, aquellos edificios*. For an unnamed 'this'/'that' with no noun — pointing at a mystery object — use the neuter **esto/eso/aquello** (no accent, no gender): *¿qué es esto?*, *eso no me gusta*. (You may see accents on the pronoun forms — éste, ése — but modern spelling drops them; the neuter never takes one.)

examples.translation: That building over there is very old.

commonErrors.note: taco is masculine → este; agreement is mandatory.

commonErrors.wrong: ¿Qué es este? (pointing at a mystery object)
commonErrors.note: For an unidentified thing, use the neuter esto.

title: Talking about time: hace, desde hace, and telling the clock
explanationMd: **Duration up to now**: *hace + time + que + present*, or *present + desde hace + time* — both = 'have been ___ing for': *estudio español desde hace seis meses* / *hace seis meses que estudio español* (I've been studying Spanish for six months — note the PRESENT tense). **'Ago'**: *hace + time* with a past verb: *llegué hace diez minutos* (I arrived ten minutes ago). **Telling time** uses ser + the feminine article, agreeing with the hour: *es la una* (only 1 o'clock is singular), *son las tres*, *son las diez y media* (10:30), *son las cinco menos cuarto* (4:45). Mexico mixes 12- and 24-hour formats; adding 'de la mañana/tarde/noche' clarifies.

examples.text: Estudio español desde hace seis meses.
examples.translation: I've been studying Spanish for six months. (present tense)

examples.text: Es la una de la tarde.

commonErrors.wrong: He estudiado español desde hace seis meses. (for an ongoing state)
commonErrors.right: Estudio español desde hace seis meses.
commonErrors.note: Ongoing duration takes the present tense in Spanish.
Plural hours take 'son'; only 'la una' takes 'es'.

drills.answer: Vivo aquí desde hace dos años.

title: Diminutives: the -ito/-ita reflex (and ahorita)
explanationMd: Mexican Spanish runs on diminutives — the suffix **-ito/-ita** (or -cito/-cita after n, r, e) attached to nouns, adjectives, even adverbs. They rarely mean literally 'small'; they add warmth, politeness, softening, or affection. *Un cafecito* (a nice little coffee), *ahí en la esquinita* (right there on the corner), *¿me esperas un ratito?* (a little bit), *gracias, muy amable, que le vaya bonito*. Crucially, they soften requests and time: **ahorita** (from ahora) is the famous elastic one — it can mean 'right now', 'in a minute', or 'sometime, maybe never', all depending on tone. **Ahí/al ratito**, **poquito**, **rapidito** work the same way. Overusing them as a learner is charming, not wrong; under-using them can sound blunt.

examples.translation: Could I get a little coffee, please? (softened)
Wait for me a little bit.

commonErrors.right: 'Ahorita' is elastic — often 'in a bit' or 'eventually'.
commonErrors.note: Tone and context set the real timeframe; don't take it literally.
The stem adjusts: poco → poquito (c→qu to keep the [k] sound).

drills.answer: 'in a minute' or 'eventually / maybe never' — it's elastic.

title: estar + gerund — what's happening right now
explanationMd: To stress that something is happening at this moment, use **estar + gerund** (the -ndo form): **-ar** → -ando (hablando), **-er/-ir** → -iendo (comiendo, viviendo). *Estoy comiendo* = I'm eating (right now). Note Spanish uses this LESS than English: for habits and near-future it prefers the plain present — *trabajo en un banco* (I work at a bank), *mañana voy al doctor* (I'm going to the doctor tomorrow). Reserve the progressive for genuinely in-progress action. A few irregular gerunds: leer→leyendo, dormir→durmiendo, pedir→pidiendo, decir→diciendo. Object/reflexive pronouns attach to the gerund (accent added) or sit before estar: *estoy haciéndolo = lo estoy haciendo*.

examples.text: Estoy comiendo, te llamo al rato.
examples.translation: I'm eating, I'll call you later.

commonErrors.wrong: Estoy trabajando en un banco. (as your general job)
commonErrors.note: For habitual facts, Spanish uses the plain present, not the progressive.

commonErrors.right: Como mañana. / Voy a comer mañana.
commonErrors.note: Don't use the progressive for the future — use the present or ir a.

title: Commands: giving directions, requests, and advice
explanationMd: **Informal (tú) affirmative** commands usually just = the él/ella present form: *habla* (speak), *come* (eat), *pásame la sal* (pass me the salt). A famous set of irregulars must be memorized: **di** (say), **haz** (do), **ve** (go), **pon** (put), **sal** (leave), **ten** (have), **ven** (come). **Formal (usted)** commands flip the vowel — -ar verbs take -e, -er/-ir take -a: *hable*, *coma*, *siga derecho* (go straight), *disculpe*. Plural is **-en/-an** (siganle, pásenle). **Negative** commands all use the flipped-vowel form, even for tú: *no hables*, *no te preocupes*. Pronouns attach to affirmative commands (*dígame*, *siéntate*) but go before negatives (*no me digas*). Mexican service is full of these: *pásele, dígame, tómelo, ándele*.

examples.translation: Pass me the salt, please. (tú command)
Go straight for two blocks. (usted command)

commonErrors.note: Negative commands use the flipped form (preocupes) and the pronoun goes BEFORE: no te preocupes.

commonErrors.wrong: Hazte derecho (for 'go straight' to a stranger)
commonErrors.note: To a stranger use the usted command (siga), not tú (sigue/ve).

title: hay (there is/are) and impersonal weather with hacer
explanationMd: **hay** (from haber) = 'there is' AND 'there are' — one invariable word, never changes for number: *hay un problema*, *hay muchos tacos*, *¿hay baño?* Negative: *no hay* (*no hay leche* = there's no milk). Don't confuse the existence 'hay' with 'está/están', which locate a KNOWN thing: *hay un banco por aquí* (there's a bank around here — existence) vs *el banco está en la esquina* (the bank is on the corner — location). **Weather** is mostly impersonal, built with **hacer**: *hace calor/frío/sol/viento* (it's hot/cold/sunny/windy), *hace buen tiempo* (the weather's nice). But some use other verbs: *está nublado* (it's cloudy — estar), *llueve / está lloviendo* (it's raining — llover), *hay neblina* (there's fog — hay). Personal cold/heat is tener: *tengo frío*, never 'hace frío' about yourself.

examples.translation: Is there a pharmacy around here?

examples.text: Está nublado y va a llover.
examples.translation: It's cloudy and it's going to rain.

commonErrors.note: hay never changes form — same for singular and plural.
Personal cold uses tener; 'hace frío' is only the weather; 'está frío' is a cold object.
